title: Lec-39: Ethernet Frame Format (IEEE-802.3) in Data Link Layer

segments.text: Hello students, welcome to Gate Smashers.
In this video I am going to explain Ethernet Frame Format.
And we will discuss all important points related to Ethernet Frame Format
which will be very hepful in your competitive exams, even your college/University exams.
So let's start. First of all in Ethernet Frame Format, what actually is Ethernet?
Ethernet is a Data Link Layer protocol. In Data Link Layer
there are many famous protocols like in Network Layer we discussed IPv4, IPv6 or
in Transport Layer there were TCP, UDP or in Application Layer SMTP, etc. many of them.
Similarly we have in Data Link Layer like HDLC is very popular, other than this
there is IEEE-802.11, which is in wireless LAN is quite popular or token ring, etc.
And similarly we have, one of the most important you can say, for competitive exams,
even college/University exams, that is Ethernet Frame Format.
So we use Ethernet in Data Link Layer.
And basically you can say this is a LAN protocol means we use in LAN technology.
And this is defined in IEEE802.3 and it came in 1983.
So it is used till now, it actually came in 1983.
And this basically uses CSMA/CD technology. So CSMA/CD we have already discussed
that is Carrier Sense Multiple Access Collision Detection, getting the point?
Because there is no scene of acknowledgements here, we are using in LAN,
if we put acknowledgement then traffic will be very high, so to detect collision
we have a methodology CSMA/CD, that technology we use here for medium access control.
Now next comes types of Ethernet. In types of Ethernet we have 10Base2,
10Base2 means this 10 is 10MBPS, Megabits per second, Base- baseband
and this 2 is 200 meter. This is called Thin Ethernet. Then we have 10Base5,
Thick Ethernet, here we use coaxial cables. Then comes twisted pair, fiber channel.
This we call Fast Ethernet which is 100MBPS, Megabits per second, and then
like we have 10 Gigabits per second also. So 10 Gigabits per second and
this is also used in twisted pair and in fiber channel also we use it.
That is called Gigabit Ethernet. These little points are quite important,
many times they come directly in the exam. And which topology is used in this?
Bus topology is used, although instead of bus, star can also be used.
But if you are asked anywhere, top priority you have to give what?
Bus topology we use here. You already know how devices are connected in bus topology.
So the same topology we use here. So in this bit rate is from 1MBPS to 400GBPS.
This was launched in 2017, speed of upto 400 Gigabits per second, data rate it can achieve.
So these little main points you can say in Ethernet. But main is the Frame Format. Physical layer adds it. But who adds the remaining? Data Link Layer adds.
So let's start. In Frame Format the first 2 fields, Preamble and SFD,
both these fields are not added in Data Link Layer, they are added in Physical Layer.
Means it is part of Frame Format, but if you are asked who adds this?
So what is this Preamble? It is a 7 byte field. Means it becomes 56 bits,
and these are constant bits, 101010... upto... means all 56 are in the 10 format.
This way it is a 1010 pattern, and this SFD is what? It is of 1 byte means 8 bits,
and this is also what? It's a constant bit 10101011, getting the point?
So why did we put this constant bit of 8 bytes in starting?
It is just for alerting the receiver. To alert him that wake up, message is coming.
Otherwise what happens is it will always be in active form, waiting when a message will come.
Instead what we did? We put Preamble, SFD, initially only SFD was attached,
only SFD would be sufficient, because we assumed that this pattern will never come in data.
We used this only to wake up, to alert, to syncronize, but as usage increased
then they added Preamble. So these 8 bytes are fixed, constant don't touch,
only Physical Layer adds them, just for syncronizing, just for alerting the receiver
that message is coming, wake up. After that we have Destination Address.
Now what is this Destination Address? MAC address. And MAC address you know
Physical Address, NIC address, and this is basically of 48 bits.
So you can say this is a 6 byte Destination Address, 6 byte Source Address,
Source Address means the sender's machine address. Means how it works here,
many times you are asked related to this in the exam, like we have source,
and this source is connected to destination and in between we have say 2 nodes,
take router or any device, bridge, switch, x1 and x2.
Now let's say source has to send packet to destination. So what will be the Source Address?
The Physical Addres of this machine, this machine's physical address will put 48 bits.
What will be the Destination Address? Destination Address should be of this,
but how will source know the Physical Address of this machine? It doesn't know.
So in case of IP Address you can say that IP Address we put directly,
of source and destination because IP Address doesn't change on the way
and it's responsibility is source to destination delivery,
but responsibility of Data Link Layer is node to node, hop to hop. So what does it mean?
Responsibility of this is just to deliver it from here to here.
So it will reach upto D later, we first have to see who is next, who is next? x1.
So here I will put Physical Address of x1, getting the point or not?
We will put Physical Address of x1, and if you are asked how do we know Physical Address of x1?
For that we use ARP packet, ARP protocol.
I know the IP Address of this gateway, this switch
and from IP Address how we can make MAC address? We will get through ARP packet.
So this works this way. When this packet reaches here, when this sends the next one,
what will it do before sending the next one? It will put Physical Address of next one
and it will put its own Source Address. This one will do what?
It will put MAC Address of destination and its own Source Address.
So this way packet will reach the destination. So this way addresses change on the way.
And this story is asked many times- Destination Address and Source.
Then comes length, length is of 2 bytes. 2 bytes means 16 bits,
means 0 to 2 raised to power 16 minus 1, means 0 to 65535
can be length of the packet, length of the frame.
Here we generally use frame nomenclature. We use packet in Network Layer,
we use segment in Transport layer, so I'm just saying packet sometimes,
but you consider I am talking about frame. Okay? Although the length is what?
It can be this much, from 0 to this much, but length is fixed in this.
I will come to length, but here you can say that many times what happens is,
many times frames are of fixed length and many times of variable length.
So in fixed length field even if you don't fill the field, it'll work.
Because in fixed you know that every time frame is of let's say 500 bytes or 2000 bytes,
so you have fixed. But in case of variable you will know from length.
Like we did in IPv4 how much is total length, similarly here length.
Next comes data. Many times question comes on this,
guys remember in this I'm talkng about pure data, I'm talking about payload.
Pure data should be at least 46 bytes.
Atleast it should be 46 bytes
Why? If it is less than 46 bytes then CSMA/CD,
guys its link is given in the description box, if you have studied CSMA/CD well,
then in that Collision Detection happens only when
Transmission Time should be equal to or greater than 2 into Propogation Time.
Means Transmission Time should be twice of Propogation Time,
and if I want to detect collision then packet length should be atleast 46 bytes.
And this is just data, I'm talking about only data.
And when you are asked minimum size of the whole frame, you know the data,
but if you are asked about the whole frame, then add in this, CRC 4 bytes,
6, 12, 18, so total is how much? Add another 18 to this,
and if you want to add Physical Layers of Preamble and SFD also, add to this,
so add 8 to this, how much it becomes? 72. Getting the point or not?
This is 64 plus 8, 72. Okay? So this minimum you can say, becomes 72 bytes.
But generally we actually add Preamble and SFD to Physical Layer,
so that's why you can say, if you add only this field,
then what will its total size be? It will be 64 bytes. 46 plus add this 18.
And if we talk about maximum PDU, means maximum size,
then maximum data should be of 1500 bytes. Pure data of 1500
and if you are asked what is the maximum frame size of data? What is the maximum frame size?
Then to 1500 you add 18, so 1518. Getting the point?
And if you are asked to add these 2 Physical Layer fields, then add 8 bytes more to this,
so this way you can calculate its total maximum and minimum size.
Guys remember this point, it can be asked in the exam. And finally we have CRC,
Cyclic Redundancy Check which is of 4 bytes, we get 32 bits, which is what?
We use it for Error Detection and CRC is quite popular in Error Detection,
comes a lot in exams. So if you want to check out the video on CRC then
its link is also given in the description box. You should definitely check it.
So this is the whole story of Ethernet Frame Format.
Okay? So, that's all.
Thank you.
